pattern_area: Hero visual (product-representation graphic in the primary hero section)
current_flagged_state: The current hero centers a fabricated stylized 'Match review' UI card that does not correspond to any real screen in the product: it floats above the page, contains an animated progress/meter element, and a pulsing status dot, all wrapped in ambient decoration (a soft glowing orb behind it) plus a continuous card-float (bob) animation. This is a close cousin of the 'fake mockup' AI tell - an invented interface built purely to look like software, rather than a screenshot, illustration, or data view that is actually true to the product. The animated meter and pulsing dot in particular read as decorative liveliness rather than communicating any real state, which is the signature of AI-generated 'trust theater' UI.
goal: Give the hero a single strong visual concept that is verifiably true - either an honest view of the real product, a real/representative data visualization, real photography, or typography alone - so the hero builds credibility instead of undermining it. The visual should read as intentional and current (2025-2026 editorial/structural hero trends) rather than templated, and should support the site's professional, dashboard-leaning tone without inventing a fake interface to do it.

options.option: Real product screenshot in an honest, minimal frame
options.description: Capture an actual screen from the working product (e.g., a real scholarship match results list or profile view), lightly cropped/cleaned (remove personal test data, tidy scrollbars), and place it in a restrained frame (thin border + soft box-shadow, or a simplified browser/window bar) rather than a free-floating illustrated card. Optionally annotate or spotlight one real feature (a callout line pointing at an actual match score) instead of inventing a meter widget.
options.why_it_reads_human: It answers the visitor's implicit question ('what does this actually look like?') with truth instead of a facsimile. Static or gently scroll-revealed - never idly pulsing or bobbing - it signals a finished, working product rather than a generated demo prop. Annotation/highlighting (rather than a fake animated status dot) shows editorial intent: a human chose what to point at.
options.adoption_examples: Stripe (product screenshot with two-column copy/visual split), Linear (real app screenshots in minimal frames), Notion (looping real-product video, not an invented card), general SaaS pattern of 'one real screen, big, in a browser or laptop frame' documented across 2026 SaaS hero teardowns

options.description: Replace the fake meter/status-dot card with a small, real or representative data view drawn with the site's own numbers - e.g., an actual distribution of scholarship amounts, a real count of matches found, or an honest 'time saved' stat rendered as a simple bar/line/radial chart using the dataviz skill's accessible chart conventions. No invented percentages or live-looking counters that don't correspond to real computed values.
options.why_it_reads_human: Charts that are demonstrably tied to real underlying data (labeled units, plausible non-round numbers, a visible source/date) read as evidence, not decoration. This directly avoids the 'trust theater' problem of an animated meter with no real referent.
options.adoption_examples: Data-led B2B/analytics hero sections cited in 2026 SaaS/data-visualization design roundups (Awwwards data-viz showcase, Smashing Magazine 'Delightful Data Visualization' teardown); The Pudding's approach of grounding every visual in real underlying data

options.description: Use a single custom (non-stock-feeling) photograph - a real student, counselor, or workspace moment relevant to the scholarship-search journey - as the dominant hero visual, paired with restrained typography and a CTA. No UI card at all.
options.why_it_reads_human: Real photography of real people/context is one of the fastest ways to humanize a product and is explicitly recommended in fintech/edtech trust-design guidance, provided it avoids generic stock-photo tropes (over-posed smiling models, obvious stock-photo energy).
options.adoption_examples: EdTech homepages using a large central hero image of a tutor/student pairing to convey warmth (per 2025-2026 edtech design roundups); fintech sites favoring real team/customer photography over illustration to build trust
options.effort: medium-high (requires a real photo shoot or a carefully chosen, non-generic licensed image; low effort only if usable photography already exists)

options.description: Drop the product-representation graphic entirely. Let an oversized Fraunces display headline (with Hanken Grotesk supporting copy and a Space Mono micro-label or stat) carry the entire hero, using scale, negative space, and a single marigold accent (underline, highlighted word, or thin rule) as the only 'visual'.
options.why_it_reads_human: 2025-2026 hero trends explicitly favor 'typography-first,' structure-over-flash heroes: oversized type with minimal content feels intentional and confident precisely because there's nothing to fake. It sidesteps the fake-UI problem entirely by not attempting to represent the product visually in the hero.
options.adoption_examples: Type-dominant editorial hero sections cited in 2025-2026 roundups (Monolith, Ransom-style bold-type heroes); broader 'structure over flash' trend of editorial grids and type-first heroes replacing decorative hero graphics

options.option: Editorial split: typography + one honest static UI element (recommended combination)
options.description: A two-column or layered hero: dominant Fraunces headline and copy on one side; on the other, a single, real, static (not floating/pulsing) screenshot or data view, held in a simple frame with a restrained marigold accent marking one true data point (e.g., an actual match count). No ambient orb, no idle animation loop - any motion is a one-time scroll-triggered reveal, respecting prefers-reduced-motion.
options.why_it_reads_human: Combines the confidence of a type-led composition (current, structural, 2025-2026-appropriate) with the credibility of a real product view, satisfying a 'professional/dashboard-leaning' preference without resorting to a fabricated card. Because the visual is real and static-by-default, it cannot read as a generated trust prop.
options.adoption_examples: Two-column 'copy left, real product right' pattern used by Stripe, Vercel, Figma-style B2B homepages; 'editorial typography + one restrained real screenshot' pattern noted in 2026 B2B homepage teardowns as the credible alternative to gradient-and-mockup templates

options.description: Instead of a picture of a UI, embed a small piece of the actual working product in the hero (e.g., a real, functional search box that returns real placeholder results, or a live-computed stat pulled from the real match engine).
options.why_it_reads_human: It cannot be a fake mockup because it is the real thing, functioning. Visitors can verify it themselves by interacting with it.
options.adoption_examples: Interactive product widgets embedded directly in marketing heroes (calculator/estimator widgets on fintech sites; live search-preview boxes on SaaS homepages)
options.effort: high (requires exposing a safe, sandboxed slice of real backend functionality client-side, plus more JS/CSP planning)

recommended: Editorial split hero: an oversized Fraunces headline (with Hanken Grotesk sub-copy) as the dominant element, paired with a single real, static screenshot of the actual scholarship-match results screen held in a minimal CSS frame (thin border + soft box-shadow, optionally a simplified two-dot 'window' bar rather than literal browser chrome), with exactly one Space Mono real statistic (e.g., an actual match count or scholarship-amount figure, set in tabular numerals) and one marigold accent used sparingly (an underline on a headline word, or a thin highlight rule under the one real data point) - no ambient orb, no pulsing dot, no floating/bobbing animation. Reasoning: this satisfies the owner's 'professional, dashboard-leaning' preference (there is still a UI-like visual anchoring the page) while eliminating every element that made the current hero read as fabricated - it is a real screen, it does not idly animate, and any motion is a single scroll-triggered reveal that respects prefers-reduced-motion. It is also squarely aligned with where 2025-2026 hero design is heading (type-led structure over ornamental flash) and is achievable in vanilla CSS/JS at medium effort without violating the script-src 'self' CSP (all interaction logic lives in one external static JS file, e.g. hero.js, using IntersectionObserver - no inline handlers).
trend_context: 2025-2026 hero design is moving away from decorative 'liveliness' (ambient glowing orbs, floating/pulsing cards, gradient badges) toward structural, editorial composition: oversized type, asymmetric grids, generous negative space, and restrained, purposeful motion (a single scroll-triggered reveal rather than a continuous idle loop). Where a product visual is used, the emphasis has shifted from generic dashboard mockups toward *actual* product screenshots - sometimes lightly stylized or annotated to direct attention to one real feature, but not invented. Design commentary through mid-2026 has explicitly named the 'AI slop' hero pattern (purple/glow gradient orb behind the headline, a floating card with a thin border and soft shadow, a pill badge, an animated meter or pulsing status dot) as an immediately recognizable AI tell, which is precisely what the current hero exhibits. What reads as current: real screenshots or data, type-led hierarchy, one-time restrained motion, and visuals that could be verified against the actual product. What reads as dated/AI-templated: ambient orbs, idle floating/bobbing cards, invented progress meters, and pulsing 'live' dots that don't correspond to anything real.
vanilla_css_notes:
Frame technique (no library needed): wrap the real screenshot in a container with `border: 1px solid var(--border-color); border-radius: 12px; box-shadow: 0 20px 40px -12px rgba(0,0,0,.18); overflow: hidden;` and optionally a simplified 'window bar' `
` above the image (three small `border-radius:50%` dots or a single thin rule) rather than a literal skeuomorphic browser chrome. Use `` with `srcset`/`sizes` and `loading="eager"` + `fetchpriority="high"` (it's above the fold) for the real screenshot; export at 2x for retina and compress (WebP/AVIF with a PNG fallback). Layout: CSS Grid two-column (`grid-template-columns: 1.1fr 1fr; gap: clamp(2rem, 5vw, 5rem);`) collapsing to a single column under a max-width media query, headline first in source order for mobile. Typography: Fraunces at a large clamp'd size for the headline (`font-size: clamp(2.5rem, 6vw, 4.5rem); font-optical-sizing: auto;`), Hanken Grotesk for supporting copy, Space Mono with `font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums;` for the one real stat/label so digits don't jitter. Marigold accent: apply to a single element only - e.g. a `text-decoration-color`/custom underline (`background-image: linear-gradient(...); background-size: 100% 2px; background-position: 0 100%;`) under one headline word, or a 2px top border on the one real-data callout - never as a background glow/orb. Motion: replace the pulsing dot and card-float keyframes with a single `@keyframes fade-slide-up` reveal triggered once via `IntersectionObserver` in an external file (e.g. `/static/js/hero.js`, since CSP is `script-src 'self'` - no inline `
accessibility_notes: Give the real screenshot a specific, descriptive alt attribute (e.g., alt="Screenshot of EnsureCollege match results showing three scholarships with award amounts and deadlines") rather than generic alt text like 'dashboard' or empty alt (it is meaningful content, not decorative, so it should not be alt=""). If any text is overlaid on the screenshot or photograph, verify contrast against the busiest part of the image (use a scrim/gradient behind overlaid text rather than trusting the raw image). Any one-time reveal animation must respect `prefers-reduced-motion` at both the CSS and JS level (see above) - this fully resolves the accessibility issue the current pulsing-dot/floating-card animation created for motion-sensitive users, since nothing loops indefinitely. If a data visualization option is chosen, don't encode meaning by color alone (add direct labels/patterns for colorblind users) and keep the SVG accessible (title/desc elements or an adjacent visually-hidden data summary). Maintain a single clear heading hierarchy (one h1 for the hero headline) and ensure the CTA button has a visible focus ring (`:focus-visible`) with sufficient contrast against the light background.
pitfalls: A device/browser frame around the screenshot can itself become a cliche if overused or overly ornate (heavy skeuomorphic chrome, fake traffic-light buttons, exaggerated drop shadows) - keep the frame minimal and let the real content carry the visual weight.

A 'real' screenshot stops being credible the moment it's staged or sanitized to look too perfect (fake names, suspiciously round numbers, no visual noise at all) - use genuine, slightly imperfect real data/content, not a curated fantasy version of the product.

Data visualizations are only trustworthy if the numbers are actually real or clearly labeled as representative/illustrative; inventing a chart with fabricated statistics is exactly as dishonest as the current fake meter, just in a different visual form.

A typographic-only hero can read as lazy or empty if the type isn't genuinely well-crafted (poor optical sizing, default line-height, no real hierarchy) - it removes the fake-UI risk but raises the bar on pure typographic execution.

Photography can reintroduce the same credibility problem in a different guise if it looks like generic, obviously-licensed stock photography (forced smiles, borrowed office scenes) - it must feel specific to this product and its actual users.

Combining too many of these options at once (screenshot + chart + photo + big type) reintroduces the clutter and 'ornament overload' that made the original hero read as templated - pick one dominant real visual, not several competing ones.

Any residual ambient motion (even a slow, subtle one) left over from the current design - a lingering glow, a slow parallax drift - can quietly reintroduce the 'trust theater' feel even after the meter and pulsing dot are removed; audit for any indefinite/looping animation, not just the obvious ones.
uncertain: adoption_examples fields cite general, well-documented patterns from named companies (Stripe, Linear, Notion, etc.) rather than a verified live screenshot/current-state audit of each site's hero as of July 2026, since sites redesign frequently - treat company names as illustrative of the pattern, not a guarantee of their exact current hero implementation.

effort ratings (low/medium/high) are estimates for a vanilla CSS/JS implementation and assume existing real screenshots/photography/data are obtainable; actual effort could shift if new photography or a sanctioned production screenshot has to be produced from scratch.
